q: According to Newton's second law, an object that is accelerating must have a net force acting on it.
Centripetal acceleration is directed away from the centre of the circle at any moment.
Centripetal force is the net force directed towards the centre of the circle required for an object in circular motion.
In the absence of a centripetal force, an object in uniform circular motion would continue to move in a circle.
According to Newton's first law, an object without a net force applied would move in a straight line.
The magnitude of the centripetal force is given by the expression $F_{c} = mr^{2}\omega$.
To swing a ball on the end of a string in a circle, the tension in the string provides the required centripetal force for the ball to keep moving in circular path.
The gravitational force of the moon on the earth acts as the centripetal force for the planets orbiting the sun.
The electric force acting on an electron orbiting the nucleus is an example of a centripetal force.
Tension force originated in a string is always perpendicular to the required centripetal force.
An attractive force exists between the sun and the earth in a direction perpendicular to the path of the earth.
The Coulomb attractive force between the nucleus and the electron acts as the required centrifugal force.
When a car turns in a circular path, the frictional force between the road and the car tires acts normally to the direction of motion.
Resolving the reaction force of the road into two components, the vertical component acts towards the centre of the circle.
On a flat road, the required centripetal force for circular motion is supplied by friction between the tires and the road.
Banking of roads is the practice of lowering the outer edge of a curved road higher than the inner edge.
The angle that an inclined track makes with horizontal is called angle of banking.
For every banked curve, the frictional force between the tires and the road is always required to supply the centripetal force.
The horizontal component of the normal force, $F_{N} \sin \theta$, towards the centre of the curve just equals the centripetal force.
Since there is no vertical motion for a car on a banked road, the net force on car in y direction is non-zero.
The banking angle of a road $\theta$ is chosen so that a specific condition holds for the limiting speed $v$.
The relationship between banking angle and speed is given by the expression $\tan \theta = \frac{rg}{v^{2}}$.
The upward thrust (lifting force) acts normally to an aeroplane body.
When an aeroplane tilts, the vertical component of the lifting force on the aeroplane provides the centripetal force.
A force is needed to deviate an object from its straight line path and accelerate towards the centre of the circle.
Frictional force between road and car tires acts in the same direction as the motion of the car during a turn.
Normal force $F_{N}$ is the reaction force of the road acting normally to the car.
The magnitude of centripetal force is independent of the speed of the object.
Centripetal force is required for a ball to keep moving in a circular path when it is whirled on the end of a string.
If the frictional force is sufficient under wet and slippery conditions, the car will skid out of the circular path.
Banking of roads provides the necessary centripetal force to automobiles so that they take a safe turn without skidding.
The normal force $F_{N}$ exerted by a banked road is parallel to the road surface.
For every banked curve, there is a limiting speed at which the entire centripetal force is supplied by the horizontal component of the normal force.
Centripetal acceleration is directed away from the axis of rotation or centre of curvature.
Centripetal force is the force acting towards the centre of the circular path around which the object is moving.
For an object moving in a circle, the net force acting on it is zero according to Newton's second law.
For uniform circular motion, the speed $v$ is constant.
Tension in a string behaves as a centripetal force even when the tension force is not normal to the direction of motion.
The gravitational force of the earth on the moon orbiting the earth is an example of the centripetal force.
The wings of an aeroplane must be banked at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal for a turn in a horizontal circle.
Frictional force acts normally to the direction of motion of the car causing the car to move in a curved path.
The horizontal component of the normal force helps the car to move in a straight line path.
The net force in the y-direction for a car on a banked road is zero, expressed as $F_{N} \cos \theta + (-mg) = 0$.
The angle of banking is defined as the angle between the inclined track and the vertical axis.
Newton's second law, $F_{net} = ma$, is used to determine the magnitude of the centripetal force.
When swinging a ball on the end of a string in a circle, the pull on the string exerts a force that acts away from the centre.
On a flat road, the frictional force acting upon the tires of the car provides the required centripetal force for circular motion.
The gravitational force of the sun on the planets orbiting the sun is an example of the centrifugal force.
An object in a circular motion requires a net force directed towards the centre of the circle called centripetal force.
The substitution of $v=r\omega$ into the centripetal force equation yields $F_{c} = m\omega / r$.
